serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 240 g) of warm honey ginger drink, as typically served in a café or casual dining setting.

nutrient_source.title: Ginger tea with honey, prepared from fresh ginger and honey in water — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Ginger root, raw (FDC ID 169231) and Honey (FDC ID 169640), combined in typical beverage proportions (approx. 5 g ginger + 20 g honey per 240 mL water, diluted to per-100 g basis)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for warm honey ginger drink in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrients estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for raw ginger (FDC ID 169231) and honey (FDC ID 169640), blended in typical café preparation proportions: approximately 5 g fresh ginger and 20 g honey dissolved in 240 mL hot water per serving. Per-100 g values derived by proportional calculation from these components. Confidence is low due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI approximately 55–58 (various floral sources); ginger has negligible glycemic contribution; blended beverage GI estimated at ~50 based on dilution and honey GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for warm honey ginger drink. GI estimated at 50 based on: (1) honey GI of approximately 55–58 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for various honey types; (2) significant dilution in water (~240 mL per serving) reduces effective GI of the beverage; (3) ginger contributes negligible carbohydrate and has no meaningful glycemic effect. Blended beverage GI conservatively estimated at 50 (low category, ≤55). GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 16.3) / 100 ≈ 8.2, where carbohydrate per 240 g serving = 6.8 × 2.4 = 16.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~2 mg per 100 g; ~5 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Warm honey ginger drink is very low in sodium (~5 mg per 240 g serving) and poses minimal concern for individuals managing hypertension, though added honey contributes simple sugars that should be moderated in overall dietary planning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived simple sugars (~6.5 g sugar per 100 g; ~15.6 g per serving) with moderate glycemic index (~50)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume warm honey ginger drinks in moderation, as honey contributes approximately 15–16 g of simple sugars per standard serving (GL ~8), which can raise postprandial blood glucose; portion control and infrequent consumption are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat; ginger has documented anti-inflammatory properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Warm honey ginger drink contains negligible fat and saturated fat, and ginger has been associated with modest anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating effects in peer-reviewed literature, making it a generally low-concern beverage for individuals with cardiovascular disease when consumed without excess honey.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Warm Honey Ginger Drink is a simple hot beverage made by infusing fresh ginger root in hot water and sweetening with honey. It is served warm and is popular across cafés and casual dining establishments in Singapore and globally. The drink has a mildly spicy, warming character from the ginger, balanced by the floral sweetness of honey. Texturally it is a clear to lightly golden liquid with no solids. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie beverage (~26 kcal per 100 g; ~62 kcal per 240 g serving) with a modest carbohydrate load derived almost entirely from honey sugars (~6.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Fat and protein content are negligible. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low category), with a glycemic load of approximately 8 per standard serving. Ginger (Zingiber officinale) contains bioactive compounds including gingerols and shogaols, which have been studied for anti-nausea, anti-inflammatory, and digestive benefits. Honey contributes antioxidants and trace minerals. Clinically, the drink is low in sodium and fat, making it broadly suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, but the honey-derived sugar content warrants moderation for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those monitoring glycemic intake. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and suitable for vegetarians; it is not strictly vegan due to honey. This record was served at Flavours by Saute - Funan via field survey data at SGD 4.30.
common_user_queries: Is warm honey ginger drink good for diabetes?
How many calories in honey ginger drink?
Is honey ginger tea low GI?
Can I drink honey ginger if I have high blood pressure?
What are the health benefits of ginger and honey drink?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for warm honey ginger drink was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared beverage. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for raw ginger and honey in typical café proportions. GI was estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database honey entries with dilution adjustment. Confidence is low due to the estimation basis for both nutrients and GI.
notes: 1. Fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — raw ginger and honey in water contribute negligible fat; USDA FoodData Central reports 0.15 g fat per 100 g raw ginger and 0 g fat per 100 g honey; at the diluted beverage concentration (~2% ginger by weight), fat rounds to 0.0 g per 100 g beverage. Fat_g is naturally zero at beverage dilution. 2. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg are all 0.0: confirmed zero by the same dilution logic and USDA source data. 3.
